headlines.title: Live updates: Opening arguments underway in Elon Musk lawsuit versus OpenAI
headlines.description: Opening statements are underway in Elon Musk’s $134 billion lawsuit against OpenAI, as attorneys for both sides lay out their arguments in federal court in Oakland. Musk was seen arriving at court Tuesday ahead of his expected testimony. OpenAI CEO Sam Altman also arrived with his legal team as proceedings began. Musk, who co-founded OpenAI, alleges the company violated its original mission to operate as a nonprofit focused on developing artificial intelligence for the benefit of humanity. His attorney told jurors that Musk invested about $38 million to help launch OpenAI with the goal of keeping its technology open source and prioritizing public safety over profit. OpenAI’s legal team pushed back, calling the lawsuit a “pageant of hypocrisy.” The company argues Musk is motivated by competition from his own artificial intelligence venture, xAI, and said OpenAI made no binding promises tied to his early investment. NBC Bay Area business and tech reporter Scott Budman is at the courthouse. He is providing live updates below.

headlines.title: Getting back on track: Muni records highest ridership since pandemic low
headlines.description: The San Francisco Municipal Transportation Agency reported Tuesday that ridership across its Muni system has reached its highest level since the onset of the COVID-19 pandemic when the number of people using public transit dramatically dropped. Muni reported over 14.9 million people used its services in March, equivalent to 85% of ridership numbers in March 2019 and a jump of 8% when compared to the same month last year. In a statement about the increase, the SFMTA said more people using the Muni Metro system have greatly contributed to the year-over-year recovery. The agency also pointed to popular community gatherings — such as the Chinese New Year Parade and Tulip Day at Union Square — as moments when ridership jumped. Last week, the SFMTA Board of Directors approved a two-year operating budget. Over $3.1 billion was approved for fiscal years 2026-2027 and 2027-2028, made possible by cost-cutting measures and the assumption that voters will approve two sales tax measures in November that would provide the fiscally strained agency with much needed funds. SFMTA staff have reported that changing travel patterns like a shift to work-from-home employment opportunities have “decimated downtown ridership.” The agency has said improvements to customer satisfaction, reliability, and creating a more efficient system will help attract more riders.
headlines.source: Andres Jimenez Larios | Bay City News

headlines.title: Mill Valley luxury estate up for sale in exchange for shares of AI company Anthropic
headlines.description: A Mill Valley homeowner is looking to dive into artificial intelligence by selling his sprawling estate in exchange for shares of San Francisco AI company Anthropic. Storm Duncan, founder of the tech investment bank Ignatious, lives primarily in Jackson Hole, Wyoming. He listed the property, which features sweeping views of San Francisco, on LinkedIn as part of an effort to reach his targeted buyer: Anthropic employees. “The Anthropic employee probably has a significant amount of liquid net worth in Anthropic,” Duncan said. “It’s not diversified and they probably don’t have a home that corresponds to their new net worth. I’m in the exact opposite scenario where I have a home, but I don’t have exposure to Anthropic, which I would like to have.” Artificial intelligence Apr 17 Making It in the Bay Apr 22 The luxury home up for sale is located at 114 Inez Place in Mill Valley’s Strawberry neighborhood. A Zillow search shows the 4,400-square-foot home features four bedrooms and five bathrooms on a hill with acres of land. The home also has panoramic views and an infinity pool. “I think one of the primary amenities of the home is that it’s 13 acres surrounded buy a lot of wildlife and land,” Duncan said. Anthropic, an AI research and safety company, could be worth up to $800 billion, according to recent reported valuations. Duncan is hoping to lure the equity holder with key benefits like keeping the upside in shares and tax advantages. “Some people think we’re in a bubble and Anthropic is overvalued,” Duncan said. “And I happen to think that we’re at the very beginning of the most transformations thing we’ll ever see in our lifetime. I realize fully that their stock could go up or down.” Lisa Smith, a realtor in the Mill Valley area, said she has never heard of anyone offering up property in exchange for company shares, but noted we are moving into a world where people are offering Bitcoin for homes. “I appreciate the seller’s creativity,” Smith said. “It’s a pretty awesome thought.” Smith said if she was advising the buyer, she would do a deep dive into the trade. “It may or may not see the same type of growth and excitement that possibly the Anthropic share is going to see when it goes public,” Smith said, adding it would be much easier if the company was trading publicly today for an “apples-to-apples comparison.” Duncan said he has had early conversation about a possible trade, but an Anthropic employee has yet to sign off on a deal. “If it doesn’t happen on the dotted line today, but you know has a possibility in the future, you know I’ll keep the dots open,” Duncan said.

headlines.title: Suspect arrested after three-hour police standoff in downtown San Jose
headlines.description: Police in San Jose successfully negotiated with a barricaded suspect and took him into custody Monday night, the department said. Officers were sent to the 200 block of S. Fourth Street to investigate a family disturbance involving a weapon, the department said. The suspect barricaded himself inside a residence, prompting street closures nearby. At 7:30 p.m., police said the suspect had surrendered and was taken into custody. No one was injured.

headlines.title: Deadly police shooting under investigation in Oakland
headlines.description: Police are investigating a deadly police shooting in Oakland. The shooting was reported late Monday afternoon in the city’s Webster neighborhood, in the 1800 block of 89th Avenue. Police said a male suspect is dead and at least one Oakland officer fired shots at the suspect. Aerial coverage from NBC Bay Area’s SkyRanger showed a heavy police presence and multiple streets blocked off with yellow police tape during the response and investigation. No other information was immediately available.

headlines.title: SFO, Oakland settle airport name dispute with new name
headlines.description: A long-running dispute over the name of Oakland’s airport has been resolved, officials announced. The airport officially will be called “Oakland San Francisco Bay Airport.” The agreement settles a trademark lawsuit between the city and county of San Francisco and the Port of Oakland over the airport’s branding. The dispute began in 2024 when the East Bay airport changed its name to “San Francisco-Oakland Bay Airport,” prompting objections from San Francisco officials. The new name is part of a resolution between the two sides, bringing an end to the yearlong legal battle. Oakland Jul 24, 2025 Oakland Nov 12, 2024 Judge orders Oakland airport to stop using new name amid trademark battle with SFO

headlines.title: San Francisco to decide on proposed water, sewer rate hikes
headlines.description: San Francisco officials are set to vote Tuesday on proposed increases to water and sewer rates following months of discussions and outreach. The San Francisco Public Utilities Commission is scheduled to hear arguments on the proposed combined rate hikes and decide whether to adopt a new rate schedule that could take effect as soon as this summer. Currently, the average household in San Francisco pays about $171 per month for combined water and sewer services. Under the proposal, that amount would rise to $189 by 2027 and $212 by 2028. The SFPUC said the increases are needed to maintain aging infrastructure and address a lack of federal funding for modernizing water and sewer systems. San Francisco residents already pay slightly more for combined services than customers in some other parts of the Bay Area, though other regional providers are also considering rate increases. If approved, the new rates could take effect as soon as July 1. The commission meeting is scheduled for 1:30 p.m. Tuesday at City Hall.

headlines.title: Flock cameras in Oakland vandalized with paint
headlines.description: Flock cameras in Oakland have been vandalized with paint as the surveillance technology continues to face scrutiny over privacy concerns. The vandalism incidents come just months after the Oakland City Council voted to expand its network of Flock cameras. It’s unclear who is responsible for the vandalism. Oakland police said they are aware of the damage and are investigating. In a statement, Flock Safety said, “When a camera is damaged, it directly impacts public safety…While Flock supports individuals making their voices heard in the democratic process, committing criminal acts should never be a part of that process.” NBC Bay Area’s Pete Suratos has more in the video report above. Alameda County Apr 22 Fairfield Apr 21 California Apr 15 Drivers sue San Jose over nearly 500 police cameras used to track drivers across the state

headlines.title: Attorney general requests review of overturned Sierra LaMar murder conviction
headlines.description: A notorious South Bay murder case may get a second look after the conviction was tossed out earlier this year. The California Attorney General’s Office is now asking the state Supreme Court for a review of a judge’s decision earlier this year to overturn a conviction in the 2012 disappearance of Sierra LaMar. The 15-year-old vanished on her way to school in Morgan Hill and her body was never found. In June 2017, Antolin Garcia-Torres was sentenced to life for her murder. Morgan Hill Mar 2 Morgan Hill Feb 27 Morgan Hill Mar 18, 2022 A judge in February overturned the conviction, partly because the Santa Clara County district attorney had used three unrelated kidnapping cases to help convict Garcia-Torres. The California Supreme Court could reinstate the conviction if it decides to review the case. It could take up to three months before the court makes a decision. Garcia-Torres has remained in prison.

headlines.title: Some Mountain View residents remain without tap water due to contamination
headlines.description: Monday marked the fourth day without tap water for nearly 70 households in Mountain View, and the residents may not be able to use water from their faucets until Friday. The city shut off water near Cuesta Park on Friday after a water main was contaminated with cement slurry. The cement slurry was being used to fill an abandoned pipeline, but it was pumped in before the pipeline was fully disconnected from the live main water system, thus causing the contamination. Mountain View Apr 26 Mountain View Apr 27 Initial testing over the weekend indicated there was bacteria in the water. The city took five more water samples on Monday and brought them to a lab for testing. Those results will determine what’s next for the dozens of impacted residents. Firefighters have been delivering cases of water to the impacted homes, and the city has turned water back on to most homes for toilet flushing only. It will be up to the California State Water Resources Control Board to determine if and when it’s safe to turn the tap water back on.

headlines.title: Former San Jose Jr. Sharks coach sentenced to 25 years for sexual misconduct
headlines.description: A former San Jose Jr. Sharks coach was sentenced Monday to 25 years in prison for sexual abuse. Kevin Whitmer pleaded no contest last year to 13 felony counts of sexual misconduct. When Whitmer was arrested in November 2023, police said the assaults happened at the Sharks Ice rink and at Whitmer’s home in San Jose. He was arrested at his Colorado home. San Jose Nov 3, 2023 San Jose Nov 6, 2023 Former San Jose Jr. Sharks coach arrested for sexual assault of a minor, child pornography

headlines.title: Kehlani returns to Oakland to celebrate new album with fans
headlines.description: It’s been a big year for Bay Area singer Kehlani, who returned to her hometown of Oakland Monday to celebrate her new self-titled album and thank supporters. Fans lined up down the street and around 1-2-3-4 Go! Records in Oakland’s Temescal district. Thousands bought the album and then waited for hours to meet and chat with the artist. The Grammy-winning singer also spoke about her impact on the local music scene. “I think it’s big for the community because so much beautiful R&B comes out of the Bay Area, and it’s time for that to be really recognized,” Kehlani told NBC Bay Area. “There’s so many great artists here that can see what I do and see that they can also do it too. I’m really proud to be part of that legacy.” Kehlani’s upcoming schedule is packed. Following the Oakland event, she is set to receive a Billboard Women in Music award before returning to tour in the Bay Area and the United Kingdom starting next week. San Jose Feb 6 Oakland Feb 2 Super Bowl Dec 11, 2025 Kehlani supports Bad Bunny Super Bowl halftime show
